is a [county](/wiki/County_(United_States) "County (United States)") in the south central part of the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut"). As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the population was 864,835,[[1]](#cite_note-1) making it the third-most populous county in Connecticut. Two of the state's five largest cities, [New Haven](/wiki/New_Haven,_Connecticut "New Haven, Connecticut") (3rd) and [Waterbury](/wiki/Waterbury,_Connecticut "Waterbury, Connecticut") (5th), are part of New Haven County.

Interactive map of New Haven County, Connecticut

**New Haven County** is a [county](/wiki/County_(United_States) "County (United States)") in the south central part of the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut"). As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the population was 864,835,[[1]](#cite_note-1) making it the third-most populous county in Connecticut. Two of the state's five largest cities, [New Haven](/wiki/New_Haven,_Connecticut "New Haven, Connecticut") (3rd) and [Waterbury](/wiki/Waterbury,_Connecticut "Waterbury, Connecticut") (5th), are part of New Haven County.

New Haven County is divided between the [New Haven, CT Metropolitan Statistical Area (MSA)](/wiki/Greater_New_Haven "Greater New Haven") and the Waterbury–Shelton, CT MSA, which are both included in the New Haven–Hartford–Waterbury, CT Combined Statistical Area (CSA).[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

[County governments](/wiki/County_government "County government") were abolished in Connecticut in 1960. Thus, as is the case with all eight of Connecticut's counties, there is no county government, and no county seat. Until 1960, the city of New Haven was the county seat. In Connecticut, towns are responsible for all local government activities, including fire and rescue, [snow removal](/wiki/Snow_removal "Snow removal") and schools. In some cases, neighboring towns will share certain activities, e.g. schools, health, etc. New Haven County is merely a group of towns on a map, and has no specific government authority. The county Sheriff system was abolished by voters and replaced by State Judicial Marshals in 2000. As a result, the state judicial system in New Haven County has three judicial districts: New Haven, Ansonia-Milford, and Waterbury. On June 6, 2022, the U.S. Census Bureau formally recognized [Connecticut's nine councils of governments](/wiki/Councils_of_governments_in_Connecticut "Councils of governments in Connecticut") as county equivalents instead of the state's eight counties. [Connecticut's eight historical counties](/wiki/List_of_counties_in_Connecticut "List of counties in Connecticut") continue to exist in name only, and are no longer considered for most federal statistical purposes going forward, even though statistical information for the county can still be created in many cases simply by grouping data from its component towns.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven_County,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Following the process of unification of [New Haven Colony](/wiki/New_Haven_Colony "New Haven Colony") with [Connecticut Colony](/wiki/Connecticut_Colony "Connecticut Colony") in 1664–65, cohesion could be improved. New Haven County was constituted by an act of the Connecticut General Court on May 10, 1666, along with [Hartford County](/wiki/Hartford_County,_Connecticut "Hartford County, Connecticut"), [Fairfield County](/wiki/Fairfield_County,_Connecticut "Fairfield County, Connecticut"), and [New London County](/wiki/New_London_County,_Connecticut "New London County, Connecticut"). The act establishing the county states:

:   This Court orders that from the east bounds of Guilford
:   vnto ye west bounds of Milford shalbe for future one County
:   wch shalbe called the County of N: Hauen. And it is
:   ordered that the County Court shalbe held at N: Hauen on
:   the second Wednesday in March and on the second Wednesday
:   in Nouember yearely.[[4]](#cite_note-ccr-4)

As established in 1666, New Haven County consisted of the towns of Milford, New Haven, and Guilford. The town of Wallingford was established in 1670 in unincorporated area north of New Haven and formally added to New Haven County in 1671. In 1675, the town of Derby was established north of Milford. In 1686, the town of Waterbury was established, but was assigned as part of Hartford County. Waterbury was transferred to New Haven County in 1728. In 1722, most of northwestern Connecticut (except for the town of Litchfield) was placed under the jurisdiction of New Haven County. Eight years later, in 1730, the eastern half of northwestern Connecticut was transferred to the jurisdiction of Hartford County. By mid-1738, with the exception of the towns of New Milford, Sharon, and Salisbury, the entire territory of northwestern Connecticut was under Hartford County. In 1751, Litchfield County was constituted consisting of all the towns in northwestern Connecticut. Between 1780 and 1807, several more towns were established along the northern boundary of New Haven County, resulting in the alteration of the limits of the county. The final boundary alteration leading to the modern boundary resulted from the establishment of the town of Middlebury on October 8, 1807.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven_County,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

According to the [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/U.S._Census_Bureau "U.S. Census Bureau"), the county has a total area of 862 square miles (2,230 km2), of which 605 square miles (1,570 km2) is land and 258 square miles (670 km2) (29.9%) is water.[[6]](#cite_note-GR1-6) It is the second-largest county in Connecticut by total area.

### Features

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven_County,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Features")]

The terrain is mostly flat near the coast, with low hills defining the rest of the area, rising significantly only in the north of the county. The highest elevation is close to the northernmost point in the county, found at two areas of approximately 1,050 feet (320 m) above sea level in the town of [Wolcott](/wiki/Wolcott,_Connecticut "Wolcott, Connecticut"). The lowest point is sea level.

Notable geographic landmarks include [Mount Carmel (*"Sleeping Giant"*)](/wiki/Sleeping_Giant_(Connecticut) "Sleeping Giant (Connecticut)"), [West Rock](/wiki/West_Rock "West Rock") and [East Rock](/wiki/East_Rock "East Rock").

### Adjacent counties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven_County,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Adjacent counties")]

* [Hartford County](/wiki/Hartford_County,_Connecticut "Hartford County, Connecticut") (north)
* [Middlesex County](/wiki/Middlesex_County,_Connecticut "Middlesex County, Connecticut") (east)
* [Fairfield County](/wiki/Fairfield_County,_Connecticut "Fairfield County, Connecticut") (west)
* [Litchfield County](/wiki/Litchfield_County,_Connecticut "Litchfield County, Connecticut") (northwest)

New Haven county is bounded on the south by [Long Island Sound](/wiki/Long_Island_Sound "Long Island Sound").

## Government and municipal services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven_County,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Government and municipal services")]

As of 1960, counties in Connecticut do not have any associated county government structure. All municipal services are provided by the towns. In order to address issues concerning more than one town, several regional agencies that help coordinate the towns for infrastructure, land use, and economic development concerns have been established. Within the geographical area of New Haven County, the regional agencies are:

* [South Central Regional Council of Governments](http://www.scrcog.org/), serving the [South Central Connecticut Planning Region](/wiki/South_Central_Connecticut_Planning_Region,_Connecticut "South Central Connecticut Planning Region, Connecticut")
* [Naugatuck Valley Council of Governments](https://nvcogct.gov/) (partly in Litchfield County), serving the [Naugatuck Valley Planning Region](/wiki/Naugatuck_Valley_Planning_Region,_Connecticut "Naugatuck Valley Planning Region, Connecticut")

### Judicial

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven_County,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Judicial")]

The geographic area of the county is served by the three separate judicial districts: Ansonia-Milford, Waterbury, and New Haven. The Ansonia-Milford jurisdiction has two superior courthouses, one in Derby, the other in Milford. The Waterbury and New Haven judicial districts have superior courthouses located, respectively, in Waterbury, and New Haven.

### Law enforcement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven_County,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Law enforcement")]

Law enforcement within the geographic area of the county is provided by the respective town police departments. Prior to 2000, a County Sheriff's Department existed for the purpose of executing judicial warrants, prisoner transport, and court security. These responsibilities have now been taken over by the [Connecticut State Marshal System](/wiki/Connecticut_State_Marshal_System "Connecticut State Marshal System").

### Fire protection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven_County,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Fire protection")]

Fire protection in the county is provided by the towns. Several towns also have [fire districts](/wiki/Special-purpose_district "Special-purpose district") that provide services to a section of the town.

Founded in 1937, New Haven County has a county-wide fire-protection agency called "New Haven County Fire Emergency Plan" based in [Hamden](/wiki/Hamden,_Connecticut "Hamden, Connecticut") to *"Coordinate Mutual Aid - Radio Problems, assist members of county at major incidents if requested, provide training"*.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

### Water service

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven_County,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Water service")]

Water service is provided by a regional non-profit public corporation known as the [South Central Connecticut Regional Water Authority](/wiki/South_Central_Connecticut_Regional_Water_Authority "South Central Connecticut Regional Water Authority"). The Regional Water Authority supplies water to most of the towns within New Haven County, excluding the Waterbury area and the towns of Guilford and Madison. The Regional Water Authority is one of only two such county-wide public water service providers in the state.

### Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven_County,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Politics")]

As with most other Connecticut counties, New Haven County is a Democratic stronghold. The last Republican victory in New Haven County was [George H. W. Bush](/wiki/George_H._W._Bush "George H. W. Bush") in 1988. From the 1990s through 2012, the county increasingly trended Democratic, though since then it has become more competitive. Though he did not carry the county, Republican [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") received over 40% of its vote in all three of his campaigns (2016, 2020 and 2024), a feat that Republican candidates had achieved only once in the previous six elections.

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven_County,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Transportation")]

### Major Roads

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven_County,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Major Roads")]

#### Boston Post Road

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven_County,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Boston Post Road")]

[U.S. 1](/wiki/U.S._Route_1_in_Connecticut "U.S. Route 1 in Connecticut") is the oldest east–west route in the county, running through all of its shoreline cities and towns. Known by various names along its length, most commonly "[Boston Post Road](/wiki/Boston_Post_Road "Boston Post Road")" or simply "Post Road", it gradually gains latitude from west to east. Thus U.S. 1 west is officially designated "South" and east is "North".

#### Interstate 91

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven_County,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Interstate 91")]

The start of [Interstate 91](/wiki/Interstate_91 "Interstate 91") begins at the interchange in New Haven with I-95. It runs parallel to U.S. Route 5 as it heads towards Hartford and Vermont.

#### Interstate 95

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven_County,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Interstate 95")]

The western portions of [Interstate 95 in Connecticut](/wiki/Interstate_95_in_Connecticut "Interstate 95 in Connecticut") are known as the [Connecticut Turnpike](/wiki/Connecticut_Turnpike "Connecticut Turnpike") or the Governor John Davis Lodge Turnpike in New Haven County and it crosses the state approximately parallel to U.S. Route 1. The road is most commonly referred to as "I-95". The highway is six lanes (sometimes eight lanes) throughout the county. It was completed in 1958 and is often clogged with traffic particularly during morning and evening [rush hours](/wiki/Rush_hour "Rush hour").

With the cost of land so high along the Gold Coast, state lawmakers say they do not consider widening the highway to be fiscally feasible, although occasional stretches between entrances and nearby exits are now sometimes connected with a fourth ["operational improvement"](http://www.fairfieldcountybusinessjournal.com/archive/011705/011705frop01.html) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20060317141129/http://www.fairfieldcountybusinessjournal.com/archive/011705/011705frop01.html) March 17, 2006, at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine") lane (for instance, westbound between the Exit 10 interchange in Darien and Exit 8 in Stamford). Expect similar added lanes in Darien and elsewhere in the Fairfield County portion of the highway in the future, lawmakers and [state Department of Transportation](/wiki/Connecticut_Department_of_Transportation "Connecticut Department of Transportation") officials say.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Wilbur Cross Parkway

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven_County,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Wilbur Cross Parkway")]

The [Wilbur Cross Parkway](/wiki/Wilbur_Cross_Parkway "Wilbur Cross Parkway") or [Connecticut Route 15](/wiki/Connecticut_Route_15 "Connecticut Route 15"), is a truck-free scenic parkway that runs through the county parallel and generally several miles north of Interstate 95. It begins at the [Igor I. Sikorsky Memorial Bridge](/wiki/Igor_I._Sikorsky_Memorial_Bridge "Igor I. Sikorsky Memorial Bridge") and terminates at the Berlin turnpike. The parkway goes through [Heroes Tunnel](/wiki/Heroes_Tunnel "Heroes Tunnel") in New Haven.

The parkway is a [National Scenic Byway](/wiki/National_Scenic_Byway "National Scenic Byway") and is listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places").[[9]](#cite_note-nrhp_fairfield-9)

#### Interstate 84

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven_County,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Interstate 84")]

[Interstate 84](/wiki/I-84_(east) "I-84 (east)"), which runs through Danbury, is scheduled to be widened to a six-lane highway at all points between Danbury and Waterbury. State officials say they hope the widening will not only benefit drivers regularly on the route but also entice some cars from the more crowded Interstate 95, which is roughly parallel to it. Heavier trucks are unlikely to use Interstate 84 more often, however, because the route is much hillier than I-95 according to a state Department of Transportation official.

### 2020 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven_County,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: 2020 census")]

As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the county had a population of 864,835. Of the residents, 20.1% were under the age of 18 and 18.1% were 65 years of age or older; the median age was 40.4 years. For every 100 females there were 92.4 males, and for every 100 females age 18 and over there were 89.7 males. 94.8% of residents lived in urban areas and 5.2% lived in rural areas.[[15]](#cite_note-Census2020DHC-15)[[16]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-16)[[17]](#cite_note-Census2020PL-17)

The racial makeup of the county was 62.9% White, 13.8% [Black or African American](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans"), 0.5% [American Indian and Alaska Native](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States"), 4.3% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_Americans "Asian Americans"), 0.1% [Native Hawaiian](/wiki/Native_Hawaiians "Native Hawaiians") and [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander "Pacific Islander"), 9.0% from some other race, and 9.5% from [two or more races](/wiki/Multiracial_Americans "Multiracial Americans"). [Hispanic or Latino](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") residents of any race comprised 19.7% of the population.[[17]](#cite_note-Census2020PL-17)

There were 343,437 households in the county, of which 28.2% had children under the age of 18 living with them and 31.9% had a female householder with no spouse or partner present. About 30.1% of all households were made up of individuals and 12.8% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older.[[16]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-16)

There were 369,178 housing units, of which 7.0% were vacant. Among occupied housing units, 60.1% were owner-occupied and 39.9% were renter-occupied. The homeowner vacancy rate was 1.3% and the rental vacancy rate was 6.9%.[[16]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-16)

### 2010 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven_County,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: 2010 census")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Ethnic_Origins_in_New_Haven_County%2C_CT.png/250px-Ethnic_Origins_in_New_Haven_County%2C_CT.png)](/wiki/File:Ethnic_Origins_in_New_Haven_County,_CT.png)

Ethnic origins in New Haven County

As of the [2010 United States census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census"), there were 862,477 people, 334,502 households, and 215,749 families residing in the county.[[21]](#cite_note-census-dp1-21) The population density was 1,426.7 inhabitants per square mile (550.9/km2). There were 362,004 housing units at an average density of 598.8 per square mile (231.2/km2).[[22]](#cite_note-census-density-22) The racial makeup of the county was 74.8% white, 12.7% black or African American, 3.5% Asian, 0.3% American Indian, 6.0% from other races, and 2.6% from two or more races. Those of Hispanic or Latino origin made up 15.0% of the population.[[21]](#cite_note-census-dp1-21) In terms of ancestry, 24.0% were [Italian](/wiki/Italians "Italians"), 17.5% were [Irish](/wiki/Irish_people "Irish people"), 9.3% were [German](/wiki/Germans "Germans"), 8.5% were [English](/wiki/English_people "English people"), 7.6% were [Polish](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people"), and 2.0% were [American](/wiki/Americans "Americans").[[23]](#cite_note-census-dp2-23)

Of the 334,502 households, 31.7% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 45.5% were married couples living together, 14.5% had a female householder with no husband present, 35.5% were non-families, and 28.9% of all households were made up of individuals. The average household size was 2.49 and the average family size was 3.09. The median age was 39.3 years.[[21]](#cite_note-census-dp1-21)

The median income for a household in the county was $61,114 and the median income for a family was $77,379. Males had a median income of $56,697 versus $43,941 for females. The per capita income for the county was $31,720. About 7.9% of families and 10.9% of the population were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line"), including 15.7% of those under age 18 and 7.1% of those age 65 or over.[[24]](#cite_note-census-dp3-24)

### 2000 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven_County,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: 2000 census")]

At the 2000 [census](/wiki/Census "Census"),[[25]](#cite_note-GR8-25) there were 824,008 people, 319,040 households, and 210,566 families residing in the county. The population density was 1,361 inhabitants per square mile (525/km2). There were 340,732 housing units at an average density of 563 per square mile (217/km2). The racial makeup of the county was 79.40% [White](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), 11.32% [Black](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)") or [African American](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), 0.25% [Native American](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), 2.33% [Asian](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), 0.04% [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), 4.51% from [other races](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), and 2.16% from two or more races. 10.09% of the population were [Hispanic](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)") or [Latino](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)") of any race. 24.5% were of [Italian](/wiki/Italian_people "Italian people"), 12.3% [Irish](/wiki/Irish_people "Irish people"), 6.0% [Polish](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people"), 5.7% [English](/wiki/English_people "English people") and 5.6% [German](/wiki/German_people "German people") ancestry according to [Census 2000](/wiki/Census_2000 "Census 2000"). 8.73% of the population reported speaking [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") at home, while 2.05% speak [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language").[[26]](#cite_note-26)

There were 319,040 households, of which 31.20% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 48.60% were [married couples](/wiki/Marriage "Marriage") living together, 13.60% had a female householder with no husband present, and 34.00% were non-families. 28.20% of all households were made up of individuals, and 11.00% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.50 and the average family size was 3.08.

The age distribution was 24.50% under the age of 18, 8.70% from 18 to 24, 30.00% from 25 to 44, 22.40% from 45 to 64, and 14.50% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 37 years. For every 100 females, there were 92.50 males. For every 100 females age 18 and over, there were 88.70 males.

The median household income was $48,834, and the median family income was $60,549. Males had a median income of $43,643 versus $32,001 for females. The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") for the county was $24,439. About 7.00% of families and 9.50% of the population were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line"), including 13.00% of those under age 18 and 7.70% of those age 65 or over.

## Metropolitan statistical area

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven_County,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Metropolitan statistical area")]

The [United States Office of Management and Budget](/wiki/United_States_Office_of_Management_and_Budget "United States Office of Management and Budget") has designated those municipalities of the county in the [South Central Connecticut Planning Region](/wiki/South_Central_Connecticut_Planning_Region,_Connecticut "South Central Connecticut Planning Region, Connecticut") as the [New Haven, CT MSA](/wiki/Greater_New_Haven "Greater New Haven"), and those municipalities of the county in the [Naugatuck Valley Planning Region](/wiki/Naugatuck_Valley_Planning_Region,_Connecticut "Naugatuck Valley Planning Region, Connecticut") as within the Waterbury–Shelton MSA. These are both within the larger New Haven–Hartford–Waterbury, CT CSA, which consists of all of Connecticut besides the [Bridgeport–Danbury–Stamford, CT MSA](/wiki/Greater_Bridgeport "Greater Bridgeport").[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

## Education

### Primary and secondary education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven_County,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Primary and secondary education")]

Education in the county area is usually provided by the individual town governments. Several less populated towns have joined to form regional school districts. Bethany, Orange, and Woodbridge are part of Region 5; Middlebury and Southbury are part of [Region 15](/wiki/Regional_School_District_15 "Regional School District 15"); and Beacon Falls and Prospect are part of Region 16.
